name: Evacuation Routes & Shelters
sourceAttribution: Compiled from FEMA Ready.gov, American Red Cross public shelter guidance, and Department of Transportation evacuation route research. Local knowledge always overrides general guidance.
license: CC-BY-4.0 (compilation); source citations carry their own public-domain license

guides.title: When to evacuate vs shelter in place
guides.sourceRef: FEMA Ready.gov Evacuation + Shelter-in-Place guidance, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
Follow official orders when they exist — they're based on situational data you don't have.

• Mandatory order: leave. Even if you've stayed before. Conditions change.
• Voluntary order: leave if you have any vulnerability — medical, mobility, dependents, no power backup, pets without supplies.

Without an official order, evacuate if:
• Your location is directly threatened (rising flood, approaching fire, lahar zone).
• Utilities are unsafe (gas leak, downed lines you can't avoid).
• You can't meet basic needs (no water, no heat in winter, no cooling in extreme heat).
• Communication is cut and you have no situational awareness.

Shelter in place if:
• Threat is chemical/biological/radiological release with shelter time advantage.
• Outdoor travel is more dangerous than staying (active tornado, deep cold, fire on access roads).
• You're advised to by authorities (e.g., hazmat plume).

For shelter-in-place from airborne hazard: close windows and doors, shut off HVAC, seal gaps with wet towels or tape, go to an interior room without windows, monitor official alerts.

guides.title: Go-bag contents
guides.sourceRef: FEMA Ready.gov Build A Kit + American Red Cross emergency kit list, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
Pack one bag per person; keep them near the door.

Water: 1 gallon per person per day, 3-day minimum.

Food: non-perishable, no-cook preferred. Energy bars, peanut butter, canned tuna/beans, dried fruit, jerky. Manual can opener.

Light & power: flashlight + extra batteries, hand-crank or battery radio (NOAA weather radio if available), power bank, charging cables for your phone.

First aid: kit with bandages, gauze, tape, antiseptic, pain reliever, prescription medications (7-day supply), copies of prescriptions, allergy meds.

Documents (in a waterproof bag): photo ID, insurance cards, medication list, emergency contact list, copies of deeds/titles, recent family photos for re-identification if separated.

Money: $100–200 in small bills. ATMs and card readers fail in outages.

Clothing: one change per person. Sturdy shoes. Rain gear. Layered for the season.

Hygiene: soap, hand sanitizer, toothbrush/paste, feminine supplies, toilet paper, garbage bags.

Special items: baby formula and diapers, pet food and bowls, glasses spare pair, hearing aid batteries, mobility aids.

Rotate water every 6 months, food yearly.

guides.title: Route planning — multiple options
guides.sourceRef: FEMA + state DOT emergency evacuation planning resources, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
Plan at least two evacuation routes from each location you spend significant time: home, work, kids' school.

• Primary route: fastest under normal conditions.
• Backup route: avoids the same chokepoints (different bridges, different freeway). If the primary is the freeway, backup is surface streets.
• Worst case: walking route. Know it.

Fuel:
• Keep your tank at least half full during high-risk seasons (hurricane, wildfire).
• Gas stations lose power in outages. Lines stretch hours.

Map considerations:
• Note bridges, low-lying roads, one-way streets — any can be cut by the same disaster you're fleeing.
• Avoid sole reliance on freeways during evacuations — they jam to a halt.
• Note location of fuel stations, hospitals, fire stations, and high ground along each route.

Meeting points:
• Have a meeting point INSIDE your neighborhood (e.g., the corner) in case of house fire.
• Have an out-of-region meeting point (relative's house in a different state) for regional disasters.
• Designate an out-of-state contact for everyone to check in with — local lines often fail when long-distance still works.

Practice. Drive each route at least once. Walk one if possible.

guides.title: Evacuating with special needs
guides.sourceRef: FEMA + American Red Cross guidance on accessible emergency preparedness, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
Pets:
• Carrier per pet, leash, food and water for 5+ days, vaccination records, current photo, medications.
• Most public shelters do NOT accept pets — locate pet-friendly hotels, boarding kennels, or friends/family beforehand. Some Red Cross shelters now have pet annexes but not all.
• Never leave pets behind.

Mobility:
• Pre-arrange transport assistance with your local emergency management agency — register BEFORE the order, not during. Many counties have a Functional Needs Registry.
• Pack mobility-aid spares (wheelchair batteries, walker tips).
• Know the location of accessible shelters.

Medical equipment dependent on power:
• Know how long your battery backup lasts (CPAP, oxygen concentrator, infusion pump).
• Identify destinations with reliable power (hospitals, hotels with backup generators).
• Have a manual / battery-powered alternative if possible.
• Talk to your supplier about emergency arrangements.

Medications:
• 7–14 day supply in your go-bag. Refresh every 3 months (medications expire).
• Carry a written list with dosages and prescribing doctor.
• Refrigerated meds: insulated bag with cold packs.

guides.title: Public shelter — what to expect
guides.sourceRef: American Red Cross + FEMA disaster shelter operations guidance, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
Most shelters are run by the American Red Cross or local emergency management.

At intake:
• Registration — name, contact, basic health info. You'll receive a wristband or similar.
• Lack of ID will NOT turn you away.
• Health screening — basic questions to identify those needing immediate medical care.

What's provided:
• Cots and blankets (bring your own pillow if comfort matters).
• Three meals per day, typically simple — bring snacks if you have dietary needs.
• Water, basic toiletries, restrooms.
• A safe place to sleep.
• Information updates.

What to bring:
• Your own essentials (clothing change, hygiene items, medications, comfort items, phone charger).
• Activities for kids — books, small games, comfort toys.
• Pillow and sleep mask if light/noise-sensitive.

Shelter rules vary but commonly:
• Quiet hours enforced (typically 10pm–6am).
• No alcohol, no firearms on premises.
• Smoking only in designated outdoor areas.
• Pets generally not allowed unless it's a pet-friendly shelter (ask before arrival).

Privacy is limited. Bring patience.
